title: Calculating Training Compute
concept: Training compute is estimated as: 6 × Parameters × Tokens FLOPs, representing the math of the forward and backward passes.

exercises.prompt: Training compute is measured in 'FLOPs'. What does FLOPs stand for?
exercises.explain: FLOPs stands for Floating Point Operations, which is the standard unit of measurement for raw mathematical calculations in computer science.

cards.title: Predicting the Future
cards.body: How do AI creators know how much electricity, money, and time they need to train a massive new model before they even turn on the computers? They use a simple mathematical shortcut to predict the future!

cards.title: The 6ND Formula
cards.body: Training compute measures the total number of math operations (FLOPs) needed to train a model. We estimate it using the formula: 6 × (Number of Parameters) × (Number of Tokens). The '6' comes from doing 2 operations per parameter during the forward pass, and 4 during the backward pass!

cards.title: The Study Session
cards.body: Think of a neural network training like reading a book. Each page is a token, and your brain power depends on your knowledge connections (parameters). To fully understand, you must read forward (forward pass) and take notes backward (backward pass). The total physical energy spent is your training compute!

cards.title: A Tiny Calculation
cards.body: If you train a tiny model with 1,000 parameters on 10,000 tokens of text, the compute estimate is: 6 × 1,000 × 10,000 = 60,000,000 FLOPs (60 Million math operations)! As models grow to billions of parameters, this number becomes astronomical.

cards.title: Myth: It's Too Complex
cards.body: People often think that calculating training compute requires running complex simulations. In reality, the simple '6 × Parameters × Tokens' approximation is incredibly accurate and widely used by top AI researchers to plan their budgets!

cards.title: Compute Breakdown
cards.body: Let's look at how compute operations are split during a single training cycle. The backward pass takes twice as much math!

right.text: Takes 4 operations per parameter to calculate updates.

left.text: Takes 2 operations per parameter to make a prediction.

exercises.prompt: Which formula is standard for estimating the training compute of a Large Language Model?
exercises.explain: The formula is 6 × Parameters × Tokens. This represents the math operations needed for both the forward and backward passes.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If you double the number of parameters in a model, the estimated training compute stays exactly the same.
exercises.explain: No, doubling either the parameters or the tokens will double the total compute, because the relationship is multiplicative (6 × Parameters × Tokens).

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing step of training that contributes to the '6' multiplier:
exercises.sentence: The 6x multiplier accounts for both the forward pass and the math-heavy backward pass.
exercises.explain: The multiplier '6' accounts for 2 operations during the forward pass and 4 operations during the backward pass (which calculates gradients to update the model).

exercises.prompt: Which of these statements about training compute is INCORRECT?

options.text: Concept A says training compute is estimated in FLOPs.
Concept B says training compute scales linearly with both parameter count and token count.
Concept C says the forward pass takes twice as much compute as the backward pass.

exercises.explain: Concept C is incorrect because the backward pass requires twice as many operations (4 per parameter) as the forward pass (2 per parameter), not the other way around.

exercises.prompt: A new AI model has 3 billion parameters and needs to be trained on 5 billion tokens. Using the rule-of-thumb formula, how much compute (in FLOPs) is required?
exercises.explain: Using the formula (6 × Parameters × Tokens), we compute: 6 × 3,000,000,000 × 5,000,000,000. 6 × 3 = 18. 18 × 5 = 90. That gives 90 Sextillion FLOPs, or 90 ZetaFLOPs (9e22 FLOPs)!
